coredata.dc:title: Novel chitosan - graphene quantum dots composite for therapeutic delivery and tracking through enzymatic stimuli response
coredata.dc:creator: Kumara, B.N.
Shambhu, R.
Prabhu, Ashwini
Prasad, K. Sudhakara
coredata.dc:description: The designing of highly efficient and biocompatible nanocomposites with multifunctional delivery and tracking characteristics is noteworthy for clinical and therapeutic applications. Herein, we report the proof-of-concept for the delivery of anti-glaucoma drug, latanoprost (LP) under an enzymatic stimulus, lysozyme (Lyz) with novel chitosan (CS) - graphene quantum dots (GQD) nanocomposite via reverse switching photoluminescence (PL) phenomenon. The LP caged CS-GQDs nanocomposite was well characterized through extensive spectral, morphological, band-gap, particle size, and zeta potential studies along with cytotoxicity assays. The regaining of PL not only confirmed LP delivery, but also facilitated intercellular tracking through in vitro bio-imaging against human corneal epithelial (HCE) cells. The AO/EB staining and biocompatibility assays further proved excellent cell viability of >80%. The successfully delivered LP protected HCE cells from oxidative injury induced by 800 μM hydrogen peroxide (H2O2). These findings justify further utility of novel CS-GQDs caged drug nanocomposite for preclinical investigations.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Chitosan
Drug delivery
Graphene quantum dots
Lysozyme
Photoluminescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Passivation and preparation of drug nanocomposite and lysozyme treatment
Cell viability assessment using Methyl Thiazolyl Tetrazolium (MTT) assay
Characterizations of surface passivated and chitosan-GQD drug nanocomposite
Particle size and zeta potential studies
In vitro MTT and AO/EB staining assay

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A. HR-TEM image, B. HR-TEM lattice fringe (inset image represents the SAED pattern), C. Absorption (Red) and emission (Blue) spectra (inset images 1 and 2 are GQDs under bright and UV light), and D. deconvoluted C1s high-resolution XPS spectra of GQD.
A, B, C and D, E, F are XPS wide survey scans, deconvoluted C1s high-resolution spectra, and HR-TEM images for GQD-PEI-SA and GQD-PEI-SA-CS-LP composites.

Represents the modification and recovery spectral studies of A. Emission, B. particle size, C. Zeta potential, D, FE-SEM image of CS-GQD drug nanocomposite, and E. Lysozyme treated CS-GQD drug nanocomposite.

In vitro fluorescence bioimaging studies of pristine, passivated, and other GQDs nanocomposites with the concentration 50 μg/mL (Magnification 100 μm, BFI-Blue field image, and RFI-Red field image) against HCE cell lines.

In vitro biocompatibility assay of A. GQD, B. GQD-PEI-SA, C. GQD-PEI-SA-CS-LP, and D. GQD-PEI-SA-CS-LP-Lyz with the concentration of 20–100 μg/mL against HCE cells. Statistical significance was assumed for p-values: ns-non significant, *P < 0.04, **P < 0.0099, **** P < 0.0001.

In vitro Quantification of the viable cell by inducing oxidative stress (H2O2) using MTT assay against HCE cell lines. Statistical significance was assumed for p-values, ns-non significant, ***P < 0.001, ****P < 0.0001.

A. Mechanism of reverse-switching at photoluminescent graphene quantum dots (PL-GQD) in the presence of lysozyme., B. Pictorial representation of In vitro intracellular photoluminescence tracking phenomenon by GQD and other nanocomposites.
Chemical reaction route for the preparation of, A. surface passivated GQD, and B. chitosan-GQD drug nanocomposite and fragmentation in the presence of lysozyme.
Surface atomic compositions (C1s, O1s, and N1s) and atomic ratio (O/C) of prepared composites were obtained from XPS analysis.

head.ce:title: Novel chitosan - graphene quantum dots composite for therapeutic delivery and tracking through enzymatic stimuli response

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nanomaterial Research Laboratory [NMRL], Nano Division, Yenepoya Research Centre, Yenepoya [Deemed to be University], Deralakatte, Mangalore 575 018, India

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Ophthalmology, Yenepoya Medical College, Yenepoya [Deemed to be University], Deralakatte, Mangalore 575 018, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Ophthalmology, Yenepoya Medical College, Yenepoya [Deemed to be University], Deralakatte, Mangalore 575 018, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Yenepoya Research Centre, Yenepoya (Deemed to be University), Mangalore 575018, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Yenepoya Research Centre, Yenepoya (Deemed to be University), Mangalore 575018, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Centre for Nutrition Studies, Yenepoya [Deemed to be University], Deralakatte, Mangalore 575 018, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Centre for Nutrition Studies, Yenepoya [Deemed to be University], Deralakatte, Mangalore 575 018, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The designing of highly efficient and biocompatible nanocomposites with multifunctional delivery and tracking characteristics is noteworthy for clinical and therapeutic applications. Herein, we report the proof-of-concept for the delivery of anti-glaucoma drug, latanoprost (LP) under an enzymatic stimulus, lysozyme (Lyz) with novel chitosan (CS) - graphene quantum dots (GQD) nanocomposite via reverse switching photoluminescence (PL) phenomenon. The LP caged CS-GQDs nanocomposite was well characterized through extensive spectral, morphological, band-gap, particle size, and zeta potential studies along with cytotoxicity assays. The regaining of PL not only confirmed LP delivery, but also facilitated intercellular tracking through in vitro bio-imaging against human corneal epithelial (HCE) cells. The AO/EB staining and biocompatibility assays further proved excellent cell viability of >80%. The successfully delivered LP protected HCE cells from oxidative injury induced by 800 μM hydrogen peroxide (H2O2). These findings justify further utility of novel CS-GQDs caged drug nanocomposite for preclinical investigations.

ce:section.ce:para: The zero-dimensional graphene has remarkable mechanical, chemical, thermal, and optical characteristics. The GQDs are formed when graphene oxide is exfoliated and has distinct physicochemical and biological features. In general, size of the GQDs are expected to be <100 nm and can be synthesized via top-down and bottom-up approaches (Zhao et al., 2018; Zhu et al., 2012). The GQDs are from the bottom-up are usually multi-layered and exhibit inferior properties with respect to graphene, whereas, the top-down approach is the cutting of bulk graphene precursors into nano-sized GQDs, which has drawn utmost importance in the field of drug delivery (Kazempour et al., 2019; Wen et al., 2016). The size-dependent PL feature amplifies GQD utilization massively in biomedical, catalysis, sensing, bio-imaging, and optoelectronics application. The GQDs found cult status than other PL nanomaterials for specific biomedical applications due to improved aqueous solubility, biocompatibility, photo and thermal stability, and the ability to transport drug of interest with bio-imaging capabilities (Chen et al., 2018). It is believed that the PL characteristics of GQDs are due to band-gap generation, heteroatom internalization, increased surface oxygen functionalities, highly active edges, and cringle-crangle configurations of GQD structures (Tajik et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2016). However, the true source of PL has yet to be determined. Previously semiconductor inorganic materials and dyes have been utilized as fluorescent materials to track drug delivery (Åhlén et al., 2018; Chang et al., 2017; Huang et al., 2018; Lang et al., 2020). Although semiconductor materials offer the same fluorescence imaging capabilities as that of GQDs, they are often believed to induce toxicity in biological systems. Moreover, semiconductor materials also tend to generate free radicals during excitation which adds to further disadvantages (Jensen, 2012; Li et al., 2011). Considering the limitations of semiconductor materials, GQDs have shown promising potential for drug delivery systems (Rakhshaei et al., 2020; Zhao et al., 2020). Thanks to the versatile properties such as non-toxic nature, tunable excitation, in vivo, and in vitro imaging respectively. Moreover, by considering the PL property, drug loading has been determined by spectroscopic emission studies (Åhlén et al., 2018; Dong et al., 2018). Recently reverse switching of PL for luminescent carbon nanoparticle (CNP) through counter-ionic ligand interactions was reported, where the caging and de-caging with positively charged macromolecules and anionic surfactant resulted in regaining of the luminescence (Misra et al., 2017). The same research group recently reported the reverse switching of PL of polymer-caged CNP under UV light (Srivastava et al., 2021).

Following the footsteps of re-switching of luminescence properties of CNPs, herein we establish the possibility of using reverse switching phenomenon of CS-GQDs nanocomposite for simultaneous drug delivery and intercellular imaging with respect to an enzymatic stimulus. We used LP (an antiglaucoma prostaglandin F2α-analogue that improves uveoscleral outflow and decreases intraocular pressure (IOP)) (Fathalla et al., 2015) embedded CS covalently connected to passivated GQDs to form LP caged novel CS-GQDs composite for tracking and delivery by utilizing Lyz, found abundant in lacrimal tear fluid (Hanstock et al., 2019). Interestingly, CS has been widely used in various applications due to its biocompatibility, mucoadhesive and cationic nature along with stimuli-responsive degradation behavior (Åhlén et al., 2018; Kumara et al., 2021; Pooresmaeil et al., 2021; Rakhshaei et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2022). On the other hand, Lyz possesses the ability to catalyze the hydrolysis of β-1,4 glycosidic bonds present in chitodextrins and peptidoglycans (Chipman & Sharon, 1969; Nordtveit et al., 1994; Vocadlo et al., 2001). It is envisaged that the Lyz cleaves the caged drug nanocomposite by hydrolyzing the β-1,4 glycosidic linkages of CS linkage, further initiates the release of LP with simultaneous recovery of PL of GQDs. The XPS analysis, particle size, and zeta potential studies, along with band-gap investigations proved successful loading and delivery of LP. The in vitro biological studies demonstrated excellent biocompatibility of the prepared nanocomposites. The reverse-switching of fluorescence has also been confirmed by in vitro fluorescence imaging studies. In addition to that, oxidative stress assay proved that, eluted LP has the same pharmacological effect akin to standard drug.

The Graphene oxide (GO, 2 mg/mL), Sulfuric acid (H2SO4), Branched polyethyleneimine (PEI, average Mw ~800 by LS, average Mn ~600 by GPC), Succinic acid (SA), Latanoprost (LP), 1-ethyl-3- [3-dimethylamino propyl]-carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), N-Hydroxy succinimide (NHS), Chitosan (CS, MW: 50,000–190,000 Da, based on viscosity), Acetic acid, Lysozyme (≥40,000 units/mg protein) (Lyz), 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyl-2H-tetrazolium bromide (MTT), and dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) were procured from Sigma-Aldrich chemicals Private Ltd. Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30%), Hydrochloric acid (HCl) and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) from Merck specialties private limited, Nitric acid (HNO3), Acridine orange (AO), Ethidium bromide (EB) from HI Media. The procured chemicals were used as such without any further purifications. The special cell culture media and other ingredients and chemicals were purchased from Thermo Scientifics and HI Media.

The optical characterizations were performed by using the instruments such as UV–Visible spectroscopy (UV–Vis) (UV-1800 Shimadzu UV spectrophotometer, Japan), Emission spectroscopy (F-2700FL SPECTROPHOTOMETER 4 J1–0004), and Fourier transform- Infrared spectroscopy with the attenuated total reflectance mode (FTIR-ATR, Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan). The surface morphological studies were performed by using High resolution -Transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM, JEOL Ltd., Tokyo, Japan) and Field Emission-Scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, Carl Zeiss, Germany). The analysis of elemental compositions was conducted using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) using Axis Ultra, Kratos Analytical, UK. Furthermore, the particle size analysis (DLS) and zeta potential by Malvern Zetasizer Ultra, and Atomic force microscopy (AFM) through Innova SPM Atomic Force Microscope. The analysis of the drug was carried out by the high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) method (Shimadzu Prominence Reverse-phase HPLC with Thermo Scientific™ Hypersil™ ODS C18 column, 5 μ, 300 × 4.6 mm). Further, in vitro studies were performed by using ZOE, BioRad, USA, and microplate reader (FluoSTAR Omega, BMG Labtech) instruments.

The GQDs were synthesized by using previously developed methods with minor modifications (Suryawanshi et al., 2014). In a round bottom (RB) flask, the 2 mL of GO (2 mg/mL) was placed, and to oxidize it, a combination of 3:1 ratio of H2SO4 (6 mL) and the HNO3 (2 mL) were added. The RB flask was ultra-sonicated for 1.5–2 h to ensure homogeneous mixing of reagent. Further, the RB flask was attached to the reflux condenser setup. The reaction was carried out at 120 °C for about 24 h, with regular stirring, until the blackish liquid turned yellowish. The formation of GQDs is observable as a yellow liquid. To extract pure GQDs, the resulting liquid was centrifuged for 10 min at 10000 rpm, filtration with a 0.22 μm syringe filter membrane (Merck), and followed by dialysis for 24 h (MWCO membrane, 0.5–1 kDa from spectrum laboratories). The resulting GQDs were freeze-dried and stored in the refrigerator.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Passivation and preparation of drug nanocomposite and lysozyme treatment
ce:section.ce:para: To create GQDs-PEI composite, 5.46 mg of branched PEI was added to the obtained GQDs (1 mg/mL) with continuous stirring in the presence of EDC/NHS (1 mg/250 μL). Further, to obtain GQDs-PEI-SA (passivated GQDs), 90 μL of SA (1 mg/mL) was added using the same condition after the overnight stirring.

75–85% Deacetylated CS (4 mg, 2% w/v) was mixed with LP (28 μg) followed by stirring for 30 min. The resulting composite was added to previously passivated GQD in the presence of EDC/NHS (1:3 ratio) to create CS-GQD drug nanocomposite. In brief, low molecular weight (LMW) CS was dissolved in deionized water containing 2% (v/v) acetic acid and agitated overnight at 1500 rpm. The CS gel was sonicated for a few minutes to eliminate the bubbles. 2 M NaOH was added to the resulting gel to adjust the pH from 3.3 to 5.5, making it slightly acidic for enhanced drug loading. After the preparation of the nanocomposite, pH was adjusted between 7.0 and 7.4. Furthermore, the resulting nanocomposite was dialyzed for a few hours with 0.5–1 kDa MWCO membrane to remove unbound LP and excess EDC/NHS. The obtained nanocomposite was further treated with 1.2 mg/mL of lysozyme for studying the drug release in the dialysis membrane.

In order to calculate the encapsulation efficiency (EE) of LP, the below-mentioned formula was used.
EE
%
=
Total amount of drug − Free amount of drug
× 100 /
Total amount of drug
. Further , quantification was performed
by
HPLC analysis
at
210
nm .

Band-gap was calculated by using the plank's quantum energy equations (Misra et al., 2017).
ΔE = hc /
λ
cut
of
where h is the planks constant, c is the speed of light, λ
cut of is the wavelength of absorption or emission of GQDs, wavelength as 372.48 nm, 387 nm, and 372.88 nm for pristine GQDs, passivated GQD, and CS-GQD nanocomposite, respectively.

HCE cells were cultured in Dulbecco's Modified Eagle's Medium (DMEM): Ham's F12 supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum and 1% antibiotic-antimycotic solution. They were incubated at 37 °C under 5% CO2, subcultured upon attaining 70% confluence, and used for the experiments after three consecutive passages.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell viability assessment using Methyl Thiazolyl Tetrazolium (MTT) assay
ce:section.ce:para: The MTT test was performed to assess the cytotoxicity of the compounds on HCE cells (Mosdam, 1983). In a 96 well microtiter plates, cells were seeded at a density of 5000 cells/well and incubated for 24 h at 37 °C in a humidified atmosphere with 5% CO2. The compositions such as GQDs, GQD-PEI-SA, GQD-PEI-SA-CS-LP), and Lyz treated drug nanocomposite (GQD-PEI-SA-CS-LP-Lyz) were added to the cells at concentrations of 20, 40, 60, 80, and 100 μg/mL, and the cells were incubated for another 24 h. Following incubation, spent media was removed from the wells, 100 μL of MTT solution (1 mg/mL) was added and incubated for 4 h. The untreated cells were considered completely viable. The absorbance of formazan crystals was measured at 570 nm using a multimode microplate reader after they were solubilized in DMSO (100 μL). The untreated cell was used as a control and the percentage cell viability was calculated using mentioned formula.
Cell viability (%) = (Optical density of the cells treated with samples/Optical density of the control) × 100.

HCE cells were seeded onto 24 well plates at a seeding density of 10,000 cells/well and incubated at 37 °C under 5% CO2 in a humidified atmosphere for 24 h. The GQDs, GQD-PEI-SA, GQD-PEI-SA-CS-LP, and GQD-PEI-SA-CS-LP-Lyz were introduced to the cells at a concentration of 50 μg/mL, the concentration at which the composite used was found to be more cytocompatible. Further, cells were incubated for another 24 h followed by imaging using a fluorescence imager.

This analysis is typically demonstrated to differentially indicate the viable and apoptotic cells (Vethakanraj et al., 2015). For this assay, HCE cells were seeded onto 24 well plates at a seeding density of 10,000 cells/ well and incubated at 37 °C under 5% CO2 in a humidified atmosphere for 24 h. Test compounds such as GQDs, GQD-PEI-SA, GQD-PEI-SA-CS-LP, and GQD-PEI-SA-CS-LP-Lyz were added to the cells at 50 μg/mL concentration and further incubated for 24 h. After the stipulated treatment period, spent media from the wells was removed and cells were washed with 1× PBS. Staining was carried out using AO/EB (2 μg/mL, 1:1) for 15 min at 37 °C under dark. Excess stain was removed and cells were washed with PBS twice. Cells were overlaid with PBS and imaged under green and red channels of a fluorescence imager. Nuclei with green fluorescence were regarded as viable, those with greenish-yellow or red fluorescence were regarded as apoptotic cells under the merge channel.

To test the in vitro pharmacological efficacy of the eluted drug by inducing oxidative stress with H2O2 (Alice et al., 2008; Yin et al., 2018), HCE cells were seeded into 96 well plates at a density of 3500 cells/well and incubated for 24 h at 37 °C in a humidified atmosphere with 5% CO2. H2O2 at various concentrations (200 μM, 400 μM, 600 μM, and 800 μM) were prepared in distilled water. To assess protective ability against oxidative injury, the same concentrations of standard and eluted LP (1 μg) were used. The control cells were assumed to be completely viable. Furthermore, cells were treated with LP for 1 h before being incubated for 2 h with various concentrations of H2O2. Further, the wells were filled with 100 μL of 1 mg/mL MTT solution and incubated for 4 h. The absorbance of formazan crystals was measured at 570 nm using a multimode microplate reader after they were solubilized in DMSO (100 μL). The percentage of cell viability was calculated using the previously mentioned formula in section 2.7.1.

The plotted values of biocompatibility and oxidative stress assays were calculated by taking mean ± standard deviation (n = 3). Statistical analysis was carried out using a one-way ANOVA analysis of variance A (Tukey's multiple comparisons test) using GraphPad Prism 8 software. A P-value <0.05 was considered significant.

The HR-TEM images displayed uniform dispersion of spherically shaped GQDs (Fig. 1 .A) with a lattice spacing distance of 0.22 nm, which was destined to be for the (100) lattice constant of the plane of graphite (Fig. 1.B). A set of hexagonal spots with symmetry points from the single area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern revealed that GQDs are well ordered and single-crystalline (Inset Fig. 1.B) (Diao et al., 2019; Lee et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2016). The obtained results of our study emphasize that; the GQDs have a few layers of graphene sheets and also invariably confirmed the ultra-small, homogeny of 3.82 ± 0.63 nm (average size) sized GQDs having crystal cores of graphitic sp2 carbon atoms (Geng et al., 2021). The optical characterizations such as, absorption (UV–Vis) and emission spectral studies were carried out further for better understanding of the properties of prepared GQDs. The band at 299 nm was determined to be for the π-π* transition, responsible for aromatic sp2 domains such as CO, CN, and CO owing to oxidation (Sun et al., 2013; Tetsuka et al., 2012), and band at 250 nm for n-π* transitions and ascribed to CC molecular structures. Further, emission maxima were observed at 372 nm under the excitation of 280 nm. The quantum dots are exhibit blue PL under UV at 360 nm wavelength whereas it is yellowish under bright (Fig. 1.C).

The deconvoluted C1s high-resolution XPS spectrum implies that GQDs have various carbon functionalities such as C-C/C=C, C-O/C-O-C, and C=O/COOH at 285.02 eV, 286.29 eV, and 288.77 eV respectively (Fig. 1.D). Additionally, the presence of these functional groups on the edge and basal planes tends to increase the solubility of GQDs and paves way for interaction with a variety of materials via π-π, covalent bond, and electrostatic interactions. Moreover, such functional groups facilitate biomedical applications of GQDs and aid in cellular penetration and intracellular localization (Liu et al., 2018). Additional characterizations are described in the supporting information (Figs. S1-S2) including surface atomic compositions for GQD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterizations of surface passivated and chitosan-GQD drug nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: In brief, the well-characterized GQDs were modified in a step-by-step process as depicted in Scheme 1 .A, for further drug delivery and imaging studies. To make the surface of GQDs more fruitful for the drug binding and to create more oxygen functionalities, PEI, and SA were wrapped over GQDs as shown in Scheme 2 .A. Interestingly, the addition of PEI improves Lyz penetration and induces effective cleaving sites along with the increased surface reactive sites for cross-linking (Kim et al., 2014). Further, the added SA was able to enhance the carboxylic functionalities. Our extensive FT-IR studies (Fig. S3) for each step of modifications clearly justified the formation of novel chitosan-GQD drug nanocomposites depicted in Scheme 2. Further, XPS analysis was employed to understand the atomic composition share with respect to each modification. The PEI-SA modified GQDs depicted the presence of C, O, and N elements with an atomic percentage of 75.01, 18.95, and 0.65, and binding energy for C1s, O1s, and N1s were found at 284.51 eV, 532.51 eV, and 401.51 eV respectively (Fig. 2 .A). Here, we observed a small quantity of N, which is due to the amination of PEI. Also, the O1s to C1s ratio was found to be 0.25, and is higher than the observed ratio for pristine GQDs (Table 1 ). These observations clearly indicated an increment in carboxyl functionalities and the availability of the same for drug composite entrapment. The deconvoluted C1s spectrum indicated the presence of 4 different functional groups such as C=C/C-C, C-O/C-O-C, CN, and C=O/COOH at 284.95 eV, 285.91 eV, 286.55 eV, and 288.83 eV respectively (Fig. 2.B). The surface morphological HR-TEM image proves the presence of GQD in the composite (Fig. 2.C), which clearly enhances the fluorescence.

The addition of CS-LP composite (Scheme 2.B) to surface passivated GQD leads to the formation of CS-GQD drug nanocomposite. Herein, an elemental atomic percentage of 56.18, 28.37, and 2.39 were observed for C1s, O1s, and N1s with binding energies at 284 eV, 532 eV, and 401 eV (Fig. 2.D). Notably, the enhanced ratio between O1s to C1s of about 0.50 for drug nanocomposite demonstrated the successful addition of drug molecules. Further, the HPLC analysis confirms 99.63% of drug encapsulation. On the other hand, a congruent change in the atomic percentage of O1s from 18.95% to 28.37% after complete modification steps, also confirmed the successful addition of LP (Table 1). The deconvoluted C1s spectrum for drug nanocomposite predominantly exhibited C=C/C-C, C-O/C-O-C, CN, and C=O/COOH at 284.71 eV, 285.25 eV, 286.17 eV, and 288.63 eV respectively (Fig. 2.E). The CN and C-O-C/C-O functional groups represent the enhanced availability and formation of N and O species on the surface and are due to the addition of LP and CS. The XPS spectra successfully confirmed the surface modifications and drug loading with an observed enhancement of O1s to C1s ratio from 0.22 to 0.25 and 0.50 for GQDs, passivated GQD, and drug nanocomposite respectively. The CS-GQD composite forms a spherical aggregated structure due to the presence of the drug, which can be clearly visible HR-TEM image (Fig. 2.F).

In the UV–Visible spectra, compared to GQDs, PEI modified GQDs exhibited an absorbance significantly similar at 299 nm, akin to previous reports on the passivation of GQDs with PEI (Xu et al., 2019). However, subsequent SA modified PEI-GQDs exhibited a decrease in absorbance denoting the effective modification of SA on passivated GQD surface. Furthermore, the modification facilitated suitable surface functionalities for the addition of LP (previously explained XPS analysis). As we can observe, the addition of composite consists of deacetylated CS and LP resulted in a negligible effect on the UV absorbance. Interestingly while compared with passivated GQD, there is an obvious change in the absorbance was noted (Fig. S4). Notably, zero cross linkers such as EDC and NHS have been used for the creation of stable therapeutic nanocomposites, which restrains the burst release and aid in the sustained release of the drug of interest for a longer period (Gu et al., 2009). As observed, after surface passivation of GQDs with PEI and SA, an obvious slight increment in emission maxima were seen due to the formation of amine and carboxylic acid functional groups. Similar results were reported wherein the addition of alkyl amine to GQDs (Hai et al., 2018; Xu et al., 2019). Interestingly, the addition of CS-LP composite to passivated GQDs led to a considerable drop in the emission maxima, this observation indeed proved the anticipated mechanism of diminished PL properties due to the formation of caged drug nanocomposite. Besides, a deviation in the emission wavelength, bathochromic shift (~18 nm) appeared for the drug nanocomposite due to the caging of the passivated GQD with CS-LP composite through covalent fashion (Fig. 3 .A). It should be noted that as in the case of luminescent carbogenic dots, the increased influence of polymer matrices was believed to afflict a stronger effect on surface electronic structures, resulting in changes of energy gaps and bathochromic shift in PL (Ding et al., 2020). Interestingly, we have observed a similar influence of local polarity on the band-gap for naked GQDs, passivated, and CS-GQD nanocomposite. As expected, in the presence of Lyz, caged drug nanocomposite degradation (GQD-PEI-SA-CS-LP) resulted in the recovery of PL (Fig. 3.A). The observed change in the enhanced intensity of emission maxima compared to caged drug nanocomposite was due to the ‘de-caging’ of nanocomposite and the presence of degraded or fragmented CS. Hence, GQDs nanocomposite could serve in therapeutic tracking applications irrespective of drugs in the form of an ‘On-Off-On’ sensor. It should be noted that CS has weak fluorescence in aqueous solution and exhibit fluorescence characteristics in its nano-micelle size after degradation (Huang et al., 2013). On the other hand, presumably enhanced emission intensity also indicated the degradation of CS and delivery of LP. Further, XPS analysis were performed to corroborate the aforementioned data with respect to the release of LP. The XPS analysis of the drug nanocomposite after release exhibited a typical survey scan for O1s, C1s, N1s, and S2p at 531.34 eV, 284.34 eV, 400.34 eV, and 168.34 eV respectively with an atomic percentage of 27.70, 57.44, 7.06, and 7.8 (Table 1 & Fig. S5.A) and deconvoluted spectra also supports the LP release (Fig. S5·B). In GQD-PEI-SA-CS-LP, we have observed the 2.39% of nitrogen content whereas GQD-PEI-SA-CS-LP-Lyz shows 7.06%. It should be noted that the Lyz-treated nanocomposites further formed fragmented CS with nitrogen functionalities, and there is a possibility for some of the Lyz could adsorb onto fragmented CS, subsequent adsorption leads to the formation of the enzyme-material complex leads to an increase in nitorgen content (Kim et al., 2018; Kim et al., 2020). Therefore, we have observed variations in the surface atomic composition of GQD-PEI-SA-CS-LP-Lyz in comparison to GQD-PEI-SA-CS-LP.

The bang gap of prepared GQDs and other combinations were calculated using Planck's quantum energy equation (Misra et al., 2017). For pristine GQDs, a band-gap of 3.33 eV was noticed and passivated GQDs showed approximately the same band-gap due to the emission maxima at the same wavelength. Whereas, the addition of CS-LP composite to passivated GQDs results in a decrease of band-gap to 3.20 eV, contributed to the bathochromic shift in emission. As observed, upon the addition of Lyz, an enhancement in the emission peak along with the shift (Hypsochromic shift) in emission wavelength similar to passivated GQD led to a band-gap of 3.32 eV. Interestingly, similar observations were reported for reverse switching of the PL found in luminescent CNPs (Misra et al., 2017).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Particle size and zeta potential studies
ce:section.ce:para: The average hydrodynamic diameter for as-synthesized GQDs was found to be 1.7 nm, with a zeta potential of −48.61 mV, inferred to be a stable product free from aggregation. Upon successful passivation with PEI and SA, an increase in the size up to 2 nm with a zeta potential of +12.19 mV was observed. The shift in the potential was noticed as similar to previous studies upon passivation (Xu et al., 2019). Whereas, the CS-LP composite addition to passivated GQDs led to a massive increase in size to 738 nm with a zeta potential of +16.16 mV. In the presence of Lyz, we observed a drop-in particle size and zeta potential to 4.1 nm and + 11.76 mV (Fig. 3.B and C). However, compared to passivated GQDs we noticed minor differences in particle size and zeta potential. This could be attributed to the presence of fragmented CS (Huang et al., 2013).

The FE-SEM study was carried out to observe the Lyz accelerated degradation/cleavage of the nanocomposite introduced any surface morphological changes. The CS-GQD drug composite was found to be flat and free from surface disorientations or disruptions (Fig. 3.D). it should be noted that, the formation of a flake-like structure with the increase in the pore size depends on the amount of Lyz treatment (Ma et al., 2014). Even, incorporation of Lyz onto poly (D, L-lactic-co-glycolic acid) microspheres in 3D CS scaffolds for tissue engineering applications wherein, the presence of enzyme leads to degradation of the scaffold by forming uneven surface, cracked and discernable ragged edges (Kim et al., 2018; Liu et al., 2012), similar structural morphologies were observed herein after the Lyz treatment with CS-GQD drug nanocomposite (Fig. 3.E). It is known that, during enzymatic degradation, an enzyme first diffuses from the surrounding solution to the surface of the material followed by the adsorption leads to the formation of the enzyme-nanocomposite complex. Then, the enzyme starts the catalysis reaction of nanocomposite resulting in the release of LP (Lončarević et al., 2017).

GQDs, passivated GQDs, drug nanocomposites as such and treated with Lyz were observed through a fluorescence microscope. As depicted in Scheme 1.B, pristine GQDs, passivated GQDs, drug nanocomposite, and Lyz treated drug nanocomposite on HCE cells under bright field have observed good morphology. Furthermore, an observable red fluorescence was noticed for pristine and passivated GQDs under the red field. Interestingly the drug nanocomposite failed to show the PL, this again proves the quenching activity observed during the PL spectral studies. However, Lyz treated drug nanocomposites successfully exhibited red fluorescence (Fig. 4 ). Moreover, the merged images clearly depict the ‘reverse switching’ and ‘On-Off-On’ phenomenon of GQD due to the decaging of the nanocomposite. In other words, successful delivery of the drug of interest, LP as shown in Fig. 3A. Similar results were reported wherein, the recovery of PL from PEI modified CNPs (Misra et al., 2017).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro MTT and AO/EB staining assay
ce:section.ce:para: The in vitro cytocompatibility of the prepared composites were examined by MTT followed by AO/EB staining assay against HCE cells. In the case of MTT, multiple concentrations varied from 20 to 100 μg/mL of GQD, GQD-PEI-SA, GQD-PEI-SA-CS-LP, and GQD-PEI-SA-CS-LP-Lyz were tested. As can be seen from Fig. 5 (A-D), all the composites were found to show good cytocompatibility with >80% cell viability. On the other hand, AO/EB staining assay (50 μg/mL) revealed higher viability of HCE cells irrespective of the GQD composites (Fig. S6). In the case of untreated control, nuclei showed green fluorescence indicate the viable nature of cells. Further, the tested compounds such as GQD, GQD-PEI-SA, GQD-PEI-SA-CS-LP, and GQD-PEI-SA-CS-LP-Lyz did not induced any apoptosis in HCE cells. The reported in vitro biological findings suggest that prepared GQDs and other nanocomposites could be widely used for biological applications such as biosensing, tracking, and imaging.

Besides, to confirm the in vitro pharmacological efficacy of the released drug, LP and the protective effect towards HCE cells damaged through induced oxidative stress has been evaluated. Theoretically, an increase in oxidative stress can also cause changes in IOP resulting in glaucoma (Alice et al., 2008; Yin et al., 2018). Herein, we used both standard and eluted drugs to protect HCE cell lines from oxidative stress caused by H2O2. As observed from Fig. 6 , the 800 μM H2O2 treated cells were observed with cell death (15–16%) due to the reactive oxygen species (ROS) production than control cells (100%) during 2-h incubation. Further, the standard and eluted LP-treated cells with different concentrations of H2O2 (200–800 μM) resulted in no obvious cell death by enhancing the cell viability (>98%). Herein, our results significantly demonstrated the pharmacological efficacy of the released drug, and also proved that nanocomposite is not altering the inherent properties of LP.

The GQDs-CS nanocomposite have the potential to be used as a biocompatible fluorescence tracker along with a vehicle for drug delivery. The as-prepared unimolecular-sized GQDs, passivated GQD, and CS drug nanocomposite is well characterized by optical and surface morphology studies. Further, the obtained results on O/C ratio and binding energy values from XPS analysis, increment in particle size, and zeta potential parameters confirm the successful modification. The ‘reverse switching’, ‘decaging’, and ‘On-Off-On’ trend of the PL mechanism was strongly confirmed through spectral, and surface morphological investigations, and in vitro bioimaging studies. The in vitro biocompatibility of the prepared composite was well demonstrated by MTT and AO/EB assay. Besides, the eluted LP showed pharmacological efficacy against oxidative stress-induced on HCE cells.

Nevertheless, continuous optimizations are required to carry out the sustainable release of LP for prolonged-release kinetics. Plausibly, the concentration of the eluted drug of interest can be tuned depending on the secretion of Lyz in the ocular region. On the other hand, rapid degradation of CS-GQD drug composite in the presence of Lyz also depends on the degree of deacetylation of CS (Kim et al., 2014). Hence, the novel CS caged GQDs could be used for sustained drug delivery systems irrespective of the drug of interest. In addition to this, by considering the availability of Lyz in the ocular region, drug nanocomposite could be used to fabricate medicated soft contact lens by initiating radical polymerization using poly (hydroxyethyl methacrylate) (pHEMA), thereby releasing the drug in a sustained manner. Since CS and GQDs are found to be biocompatible and non-toxic, retention of the same on the ocular region in post-drug delivery also assures safety for users (Pooresmaeil & Namazi, 2020). Notably, CS is a mucoadhesive and polycationic biomaterial having the ability to bind with the negatively charged mucin layer of the cornea for the prolonged release of drugs (Kumara et al., 2021). Hence, the composition of the CS, drug, and GQDs tend to act as a versatile active material for good ocular suspension, eye drop, and soft lens fabrication. Therefore, our study opens new avenues to explore more on CS-GQDs embedded nanocomposite/nanogel for simultaneous enzyme triggered therapeutic delivery, tracking, non-invasive therapies (Justin & Chen, 2018), and personalized medicated contact lens.

BNK: All Experiments, morphological studies, Fabrication of nanocomposites, Data analysis, cell culture experiments, Writing -original draft, Investigation. RS: cell culture experiments, Writing-review AP: Cell Culture experiments. KSP: Conceptualization; Funding acquisition; Supervision; Data analysis, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing; Methodology; Investigation, project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are thankful to the Department of Science and Technology, Govt of India (DST-SERB-CRG-2018/000338) for the funding support. Part of the work has been submitted in the form of an Indian Patent application (202041056414).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbpol.2022.119426.
